title: Deployment & Performance
description: Production builds, deno compile binaries, asset locking and performance middleware

blocks.text: deno task build runs dev.ts in build mode (builder.build()): bundles pages, CSS and the server entry into dist/, prerenders SSG routes, and regenerates the HTTP client. deno task start runs it.

deno task build
deno task start

Under the Vue engine, the build precompiles every .vue page chain into an importable SSR module that the snapshot statically imports — so the production server (and a deno compile binary) never needs .vue source on disk, and never loads the Vue compiler at runtime.
deno task compile wraps deno compile into a single executable — runtime, code, and (fullstack) static assets baked in. Ship to bare metal or a scratch image, no Deno install needed.
The fullstack compile task passes --include dist/static so the binary serves your built assets directly. Backend-only apps skip the include.
Two shapes work well: run the built JS with the official Deno image, or compile to a binary and drop it into a minimal base. The compiled binary keeps the image tiny.

FROM denoland/deno:latest AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN deno task build

FROM denoland/deno:latest
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["run", "-A", "dist/compiled-entry.js"]

Each build gets a unique BUILD_ID. Hydration chunks are served immutable (Cache-Control: max-age=31536000) — safe because the BUILD_ID is in their URL, so a new deploy rotates every URL and busts the cache. The id is a UUID locally, or DENO_DEPLOYMENT_ID / GITHUB_SHA in CI/Deploy.

Run your own static assets (images, fonts) through asset() to carry the BUILD_ID and earn the same immutable cache; assetSrcSet() does it for a whole srcset. Server-only — call from render/SSR, not client bundles.
Three built-ins do most of the heavy lifting in production. Register them high in the chain.

rows: Collapses a thundering herd of identical concurrent GETs into one handler execution.
Gzip/deflate for text, JSON, JS and SVG responses.
Serves static/, applying immutable caching to locked assets.

blocks.text: Render modes are the bigger lever: AOT (__page) avoids server round-trips on navigation; SSG (___page) serves prerendered HTML straight from the snapshot. See Routing.

Deploy sets DENO_DEPLOYMENT_ID, which Howl uses as the BUILD_ID — asset URLs and cache busting line up per revision automatically. For multiple instances, point rateLimitCache at a shared backend (Redis/Deno KV); per-instance counters let the limit scale with instance count.

Build before serving in production: a production server with no build output throws "Could not find _howl directory. Did you forget to run deno task build?". Run deno task build (or compile) as part of your deploy.
